claims.claim_text: SRE was founded at Google and later embraced by a broader reliability engineering community.
claims.rationale: This Google Cloud post directly says Google's SRE practice has been embraced and extended by a global community.

claims.claim_text: Ben Treynor Sloss described SRE as beginning when he joined Google in 2003 to run a Production Team, framing SRE as software engineers designing an operations team.
claims.rationale: The exact year, origin story, and quote are backed by the Google SRE book page, but `sre.google` is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Google SRE practice aims to keep toil or operational work below 50% so the remaining time can be spent on engineering work.
claims.span_hint: section: The 50% Rule; section: Did You Know #1
claims.rationale: The Google Cloud post explicitly states that within Google SRE the target is to keep toil below 50% of each SRE's time.

claims.claim_text: A core SRE principle is that 100% reliability is usually the wrong target and reliability goals should be set around user needs and tradeoffs.
claims.span_hint: section: Embrace Risk; section: Key Takeaways #3
claims.rationale: The Google Cloud Architecture Center page directly states that a goal of 100% reliability is often not the most effective strategy.

claims.claim_text: The exact 99.99%-versus-99% comparison about users not noticing extra reliability uses illustrative numbers rather than a source-backed end-to-end measurement.
claims.anchor_text: 1. **Users can't tell**: If your service is 99.99% reliable but the user's ISP is 99% reliable, they won't notice your extra 9s
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **Users may not notice**: If other parts of the end-to-end path are less reliable than your service, pushing for additional 9s may yield little visible improvement
claims.rationale: The precise percentages are illustrative teaching numbers, not a cited measurement for a real service path.

claims.claim_text: The phrase 'class SRE implements interface DevOps' comes from Google's SRE Workbook as a concise way to explain the relationship between SRE and DevOps.
claims.span_hint: section: SRE vs DevOps vs Platform Engineering
claims.rationale: The exact phrase is on the SRE Workbook page, but the primary source host is outside the allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The statement that the term 'error budget' was invented at Google is a specific origin claim that is not backed here by an allowlisted primary source.
claims.anchor_text: 2. **The term "error budget" was invented at Google** to flip the conversation from "minimize all errors" to "how much error can we afford to ship features faster?"
claims.suggested_rewrite: 2. **Error budgets became a core Google SRE concept** for balancing reliability work against feature delivery.
claims.rationale: The teaching point is sound, but the exact invention claim is stronger than the readily citable allowlisted evidence.

claims.claim_text: The Netflix example is a specific real-company organizational claim that should not stay as an uncited named anecdote.
claims.anchor_text: 3. **Netflix famously has no SRE team**. Instead, they practice "full-cycle developers" where each team owns their entire service lifecycle. They invest heavily in tooling to make this possible.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **Some organizations avoid a separate SRE function** and instead use a "you build it, you run it" or full-cycle developer model, supported by strong internal tooling.
claims.rationale: This keeps the organizational lesson while removing an uncited company-specific assertion.

claims.claim_text: The exact claim that the first Google SRE book was downloaded over 1 million times in its first month is an unsupported specific statistic.
claims.anchor_text: 4. **The first Google SRE book (2016) was downloaded over 1 million times** in its first month and sparked an industry-wide transformation. Before this, operations was rarely discussed with engineering rigor—now SRE is one of the most sought-after specializations in tech.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **When Google published the first SRE book in 2016, it helped push SRE ideas into the broader DevOps conversation.** Operations became much more widely discussed in explicit engineering terms.
claims.rationale: The precise download count is not backed by a reliable allowlisted source, but the broader influence claim can be preserved without the false precision.

claims.claim_text: The opening of the war story presents an uncited first-person anecdote as a real event.
claims.anchor_text: A startup I worked with was growing fast. Their ops team was drowning:
claims.suggested_rewrite: A fast-growing company can end up in a situation like this:
claims.rationale: This removes the unsupported first-person real-event framing while preserving the teaching setup.

claims.claim_text: The war-story staffing ratio is an unsupported precise anecdotal number.
claims.anchor_text: - 3 people handling all production for 50 developers
claims.suggested_rewrite: - A small operations group handling production for a much larger engineering organization
claims.rationale: The exact headcount ratio is uncited anecdotal precision.

claims.claim_text: The war-story nightly-call claim is an unsupported specific anecdotal operational detail.
claims.anchor_text: - On-call meant actual calls every night
claims.suggested_rewrite: - On-call interruptions were frequent and exhausting
claims.rationale: This keeps the operational lesson while removing an uncited claim about nightly frequency.

claims.claim_text: The two-week deployment-queue claim is an unsupported specific anecdotal metric.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Deployments were backed up long enough to slow delivery noticeably
claims.rationale: The exact queue duration is false precision without a source.

claims.claim_text: The six-month outcome metric for incident reduction is an unsupported specific anecdotal statistic.
claims.rationale: The percentage improvement is a specific statistic attached to an uncited anecdote.

claims.claim_text: The six-month outcome claim about unchanged team size is an unsupported specific anecdotal number.
claims.rationale: The exact headcount is uncited anecdotal precision.

claims.claim_text: The six-month outcome claim about supporting 100 developers is an unsupported specific anecdotal number.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Coverage: Able to support a larger engineering organization
claims.rationale: The exact coverage number is uncited anecdotal precision.

further_reading.title: Google Cloud: Site Reliability Engineering
further_reading.why_relevant: A current Google overview of SRE concepts, tools, and entry points for deeper study.

further_reading.title: How SRE Teams Are Organized, and How to Get Started
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful follow-up for the team-structure section because it walks through common SRE organizational models and their tradeoffs.

further_reading.title: The Systems Engineering Side of Site Reliability Engineering
further_reading.why_relevant: A foundational article on the systems-engineering mindset behind SRE and how it differs from purely software-focused roles.

notes: The shared section pool is focused on Prometheus and Kubernetes autoscaling topics and did not directly support this introductory SRE history and concepts module, so no pool source_ids were used.
